title: Fiscal policy: foundational concepts
description: In this exercise, review the purpose and intent of fiscal policy to get you warmed up for more challenging questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
The government of Hamsterville recently lowered taxes. 

**What kind of policy is this, and what is one reason the government might want to implement such a policy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: An expansionary fiscal policy to decrease unemployment
Contractionary fiscal policy to decrease unemployment
Expansionary monetary policy to decrease unemployment
Contractionary monetary policy to increase unemployment
Expansionary fiscal policy to increase unemployment

assessment_items.question_content:
After reviewing economic data, the government of Hamiltonia decided to raise taxes.

**What kind of policy is this, and what is a reason the government might want to implement such a policy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A contractionary fiscal policy to combat inflation
Expansionary fiscal policy to lower unemployment
Expansionary monetary policy to decrease unemployment
Contractionary monetary policy to increase unemployment
Contractionary fiscal policy to decrease unemployment

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a reason a government might increase government spending?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It is concerned that the unemployment rate is too high.
It is concerned that the rate of inflation is too high.
It is concerned that the level of output is too high.
It wants to lower the price level.
The economy is operating beyond its production possibilities curve (PPC).

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is the tax multiplier always less than the spending multiplier?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Changes in government spending affect aggregate demand directly but changes in taxes affect aggregate demand indirectly.
An increase in government spending leads to a smaller increase in output than a tax cut
When government spending increases, the deficit increases
Changes in government spending affect aggregate supply directly, but changes in taxes affect aggregate demand directly.
Because changes in taxes affect the money supply but changes in government spending do not

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes why an increase in government spending increases aggregate demand directly but decreases in taxes affect aggregate demand indirectly?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Government spending is included as a component of aggregate demand, but it also affects consumption. Taxes only affect consumption.
Increasing government spending increases output, but decreasing taxes lowers output.
The tax multiplier is always bigger than the government spending multiplier.
Decreases in taxes suffer from lags, but increases in government spending do not.
Increases in government spending change aggregate demand (AD), while decreases in taxes change the money supply.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following statements about fiscal policy is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A change in government spending impacts aggregate demand (AD) directly, but transfer payments affect AD indirectly.
The government spending multiplier is less than the tax multiplier.
Transfer payments are not counted in government spending, so they have no effect on output.
Government spending changes only the government spending category of aggregate demand (AD), and transfer payments change only the consumption category of AD.
Government spending does not suffer from lags, but transfer payments do.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What effect does an increase in government spending have on the AD-AS model in the short run?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Aggregate demand (AD) increases
Short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) decreases
Long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) increases
Short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) increases
Aggregate demand (AD) decreases

assessment_items.question_content:

assessment_items.question_content:
The government of Burginville passed a bill giving tax cuts to all citizens. But, by the time the government of Burginville got around to issuing tax cuts, the recession in Burginville had ended. As a result, the economy of Burginville experienced unanticipated inflation.

**Which of the following best describes the problem that Burginville experienced in this scenario?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Implementation lag
Decision lag
Data lag
Effectiveness lag
Recognition lag

assessment_items.question_content:
The economy of Burginville is suffering from a deep and prolonged recession. The government of Burginville and the central bank of Burginville are currently in talks to figure out whether to decrease taxes, increase government spending, or increase the money supply.

**Which of the following best describes the problem that Burginville is experiencing in this scenario?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Decision lag
Implementation lag
Data lag
Effectiveness lag
Recognition lag

assessment_items.question_content:
The Census Bureau in Jarminland only collects unemployment data once per year.  As a result, it was 8 months until Jarminland realized that a recession had started. 

**Which of the following best describes the problem that Jarminland is experiencing in this scenario?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Data lag
Decision lag
Implementation lag
Effectiveness lag
Outside lag
